---
title: "Section 13: One-Sample Mean Inference"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, error = TRUE)
```

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
```

## Sampling distribution of mean

### Data tidying

`cat_breeds_clean.csv` contains data on characteristics of domestic cats of different breeds.

```{r}
cats <- read_csv("../../data/cat_breeds_clean.csv")
```

### Data exploration

Let's look at the breed and sex distribution of the cats in the data.

```{r}
cats %>% 
ggplot(aes(x = Sex)) +
  geom_bar() +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("# of Records") +
  ggtitle("Cat Breeds") +
  facet_wrap(~Breed)
```


```{r}
cats %>%
ggplot(aes(x = Sex, fill = Fixed)) +
  geom_bar() +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("# of Records") +
  ggtitle("Cat Breeds") +
  facet_wrap(~Breed)
```

Let's focus on a single group: adult male fixed ragdoll cats.

```{r}
cats_small <- cats %>% 
  filter(Age_in_years >= 1) %>%
  filter(Sex == "male", Breed == "Ragdoll", Fixed == T)

nrow(cats_small)

ggplot(cats_small, aes(x = Sleep_time_hours))+
  geom_histogram() +
  ggtitle("Daily Sleep, Adult Male (fixed) Ragdoll Cats")
```



Let's visualize the sampling distribution of a sample mean.

```{r}
# Create a fake "population" of sleep hours
set.seed(3421)
pretend_pop <- c(2*rbeta(400, 1, 3) + 12, 10*rbeta(2000, 2, 5) + 14)

ggplot(mapping = aes(x = pretend_pop)) +
  geom_density() +
  labs(title = "Population of Sleep Hours")
```

```{r}
random_means <- tibble(x = replicate(n = 10000, 
                       mean(sample(pretend_pop, 135, replace = T)))
                                    )
                       

ggplot(random_means) +
  geom_histogram(aes(x = x),
                 color = "black", fill = "dodgerblue") +
  labs(title = "Sampling Distribution of Sample Mean, n = 135")
```

It has a normal shape, centered at the original population mean.  In our case, the observed mean is $\bar{x} = 16.02$.

```{r}
cats_summary <- cats_small %>%
  summarize(averageTime = mean(Sleep_time_hours),
            sdTime = sd(Sleep_time_hours),
            n = n())

cats_summary
```


## CI for mean

Let's save the summary values needed to build a CI.

```{r}
xbar <- cats_summary$averageTime
s <- cats_summary$sdTime
n <- cats_summary$n

# Estimated standard error
se <- s/sqrt(n)

# T critical value for 95% confidence
cv <- qt(0.975, df = n - 1)

# 95% CI
c(xbar - cv*se, xbar + cv*se)
```

We can also do this automatically with the `t.test()` command.  The default behavior is to output a 95% CI, but we can change this.

```{r}
t.test(cats_small$Sleep_time_hours, conf.level = 0.95)
```


## Hypothesis test for mean

Our p-value is P(T <= -4.93) + P(T >= 4.93) on the T with n-1 degrees of freedom, found with `pt()`.

```{r}
# Calculate test statistic based on observed data
test_stat <- (xbar - 17) / (s/sqrt(n))
test_stat

# Find two-sided p-value
2*pt(test_stat, df = n-1)
```

We can check our result with `t.test` by specifying the value under the null.

```{r}
t.test(cats_small$Sleep_time_hours, conf.level = 0.95)

t.test(cats_small$Sleep_time_hours, mu = 17, conf.level = 0.95)
```

We can also use a rejection region approach.

```{r}
qt(0.025, df = n-1)
qt(0.975, df = n-1)
```



### Try it out

Consider adult (fixed) female ragdoll cats.  Do we have evidence that their average daily sleep time is less than 17 hours?  How can we check this with `t.test()`?

```{r}
cats_activity <- cats %>%
  filter(Age_in_years >= 1) %>%
  filter(Breed == "Ragdoll", Sex == "female", Fixed == T)


cats_summary <- cats_activity %>%
  summarize(averageTime = mean(Sleep_time_hours),
            sdTime = sd(Sleep_time_hours),
            n = n())

cats_summary
```

```{r}
xbar <- cats_summary$averageTime
s <- cats_summary$sdTime
n <- cats_summary$n

test_stat <- (xbar - 17) / (s / sqrt(n))
test_stat


pt(test_stat, df = n-1)
```
```{r}
t.test(cats_activity$Sleep_time_hours, mu = 17, alternative = "less")
```
